text: The robotic camera view shows the left side with clips from a previous laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair, and no recurrent hernia is visible. The surgeon identifies a direct inguinal hernia defect on the right side, with no indirect hernia present. The peritoneal flap is about to be created.

The surgeon creates a large flap of the peritoneum, the thin lining of the abdominal cavity, to reduce the hernia sac and its contents. The camera view is oriented towards the right groin from above the belly button, showing the inferior epigastric blood vessels at the top of the screen. The direct inguinal hernia defect is visible on the medial aspect of these vessels in a young, healthy male.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the peritoneal flap, revealing the gonadal vessels on the right side. These vessels are visible extending from the bottom to the top of the screen, passing through the inguinal canal to supply blood to the right testicle.

On the right side of the screen, the vas deferens is visible as a white tubular structure entering the inguinal canal from a different direction. The surgeon reduces the hernia contents, revealing a small direct defect containing a large amount of incarcerated preperitoneal fat, which appears scarred into the defect.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to gently separate scar tissue, allowing the hernia contents to be reduced back into the abdomen. The dissection continues behind the pubic bone on the right side, in front of the bladder, creating space for prosthetic mesh placement to cover the hernia defect and other vulnerable areas of the groin.

The surgeon uses robotic instrumentation to reduce a small cord lipoma along the spermatic cord, visible in high-resolution three-dimensional imagery. The excess fat is minimal and unlikely to cause discomfort.

The surgeon finalizes the dissection, ensuring the space is adequate for mesh placement. A polypropylene mesh is positioned over the hernia defect in the right groin, designed to contour to the anatomical structures. The mesh is fixated with three sutures to prevent shifting, using robotic assistance for precise placement.

The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap to exclude the mesh from the abdominal cavity, ensuring it does not contact the bowel. The hernia sac is visible, and CO₂ is pushed out from the pocket to confirm the mesh lays flat.
